Many major rock and industrial artists have released deluxe editions with session material, but few waited 17 years to do so with this level of curatorial care. Taken represents the depth of material Reznor generated and chose not to share at the time.

nin-30-15.production: La Mer's alternate version reworks one of The Fragile's most otherworldly and serene pieces — a track featuring Lisa Germano's violin and a distinctly oceanic, drift-music quality. The alternate strips or rearranges elements to offer a different angle on its shimmering, tidal sound design. Where the original felt like a dreamed coastline, this version may present the same geography with different lighting — colder, more skeletal, or conversely more exposed and raw.
nin-30-15.lyrics: La Mer on The Fragile is primarily atmospheric and instrumental, with only minimally present wordless or textural vocal elements, meaning this alternate's lyrical shift is largely a compositional one. The title — French for 'the sea' — anchors both versions in the same elemental imagery. The alternate allows the harmonic and melodic material to speak through a different lens without changing the essential theme of submersion and dissolution.
nin-30-15.live: La Mer was occasionally used as transitional or atmospheric material in live contexts during The Fragile era, though its delicate construction made full live recreation difficult. The alternate version, with potentially stripped arrangement, might actually be more stageable than the original. Either version sits at the contemplative end of the NIN live spectrum.
nin-30-15.cultural: La Mer stands as one of the most genuinely beautiful and anomalous pieces in the NIN catalog — a song that seems to come from somewhere far outside industrial rock's usual emotional register. Its inclusion in Deviations signals that Reznor regarded it as a compositional cornerstone worth revisiting, not just a curio. The alternate version adds another dimension to one of the record's most loved and unusual moments.

nin-30-16.production: Stripping vocals from The Great Below reveals an immense and devastating orchestral-electronic construction that carries enormous weight without a single word. The original's string arrangements and processed piano are given even more space in the mix when Reznor's lead vocal is removed, making the instrumental a masterclass in atmospheric depth. The Nothing Studios production — warm, layered, almost cavernous — feels even more pronounced when the listener's attention is freed from following lyrics.
nin-30-16.lyrics: The Great Below originally closed The Fragile's second disc with imagery of surrender to an oceanic underworld — one of the album's most hauntingly literal expressions of suicidal ideation framed as beauty. Without those words, the instrumental version becomes a vehicle for that same emotional truth without narrating it. The music alone communicates the sensation of sinking without resistance, which is arguably more viscerally affecting.
nin-30-16.live: The Great Below was performed live during The Fragile tour as one of the set's most emotionally climactic moments, with full orchestral backing adding to its gravity. The instrumental version as heard on Deviations would function differently in a live setting — possibly even more powerfully as an extended, wordless closer. Its absence of lyrical anchor might make the arrangement feel both more abstract and more universally resonant to a crowd.
nin-30-16.cultural: The Great Below holds a significant place in the canon of NIN's most emotionally direct and vulnerable work — a song explicitly about the pull of self-destruction rendered in near-classical beauty. The instrumental version on Deviations arrives 17 years later at a moment when Reznor had publicly discussed his recovery and sobriety, adding a retrospective weight to hearing the composition without its confessional words. It becomes a document of where he was, filtered through who he became.

nin-30-17.production: Not What It Seems Like is another Deviations-exclusive track not found on The Fragile proper, its instrumental designation suggesting a composition that was developed primarily as an arrangement without a strong vocal identity. The production likely carries the moody, mid-tempo density of other Fragile-era instrumentals — synthesizer textures, programmed drums, and layered guitars in service of atmosphere over song structure. Its existence as an instrumental exclusive positions it somewhere between a proper composition and an extended mood piece.
nin-30-17.lyrics: As a fully instrumental track, Not What It Seems Like communicates through texture and movement rather than words — though the title itself carries an interesting suggestiveness about deceptive appearances or false surfaces. In The Fragile's emotional world of dissociation and unreality, that title resonates with themes of not being able to trust one's own perception. The music must carry whatever narrative the title implies.
nin-30-17.live: This track has no live history and was unknown before the 2016 box set release, meaning it exists purely as a recorded artifact of the Fragile sessions. Any live arrangement would be entirely hypothetical. Its instrumental nature makes it more malleable for reimagining in a concert context than a song built around a specific vocal performance.
nin-30-17.cultural: The title Not What It Seems Like gestures at a recurring Fragile-era motif — the idea that surfaces are unreliable, that what looks like recovery or stability may be something else entirely. Released in 2016 alongside other Deviations material, it arrived in a cultural moment where NIN's legacy was being reassessed through reissues and retrospectives, adding another layer of 'things being other than they appear.' It's a small but interesting archival piece in the larger Fragile mythology.

nin-30-18.production: White Mask is a Deviations vault track not on the original Fragile, its title conjuring imagery consistent with the album's themes of numbness, erasure, and self-concealment. The production would sit within the same 1999 Nothing Studios sonic universe — layered textures, likely subdued dynamics given the blank-face imagery. It may function more as an ambient or transitional piece than a fully developed song, which could explain its omission from the original sequencing.
nin-30-18.lyrics: Without any pre-existing lyrical context, White Mask reads as a title rich with meaning: the blank face, the hiding of identity, the performance of normalcy over pain. These were central Fragile-era preoccupations for Reznor, who was deeply struggling during the album's creation. Whether the track has vocals or is entirely instrumental, the title alone situates it firmly within that emotional landscape.
nin-30-18.live: White Mask has never been performed live and its release on Deviations marks its public debut seventeen years after recording. Its title and probable atmospheric character suggest it would work in a quiet, introspective section of a live show rather than as a propulsive set piece. It remains purely a studio document.
nin-30-18.cultural: The mask as metaphor — hiding one's true emotional state, performing wellness or normalcy — is deeply relevant to The Fragile's thematic core and to Reznor's personal history during that period of addiction and depression. White Mask as a title doubles down on that imagery in a way the final album sequencing apparently didn't need. Its 2016 excavation adds another piece to the emotional archaeology of that era.

nin-30-19.production: Removing the layered, aggressive vocal performance from 10 Miles High exposes the track's dense rhythmic and textural architecture, built on relentless propulsion and industrial tension. The original's harsh vocal attack was a key dramatic element, so the instrumental version rebalances the track toward its mechanical, grinding foundation. This shift makes the song feel more like a purely kinetic machine — relentless movement without a human point of focus.
nin-30-19.lyrics: 10 Miles High's original lyrics dealt with disconnection, emotional numbness, and the altered perception of extreme dissociation — the sensation of observing one's life from an impossible remove. Without those words, the instrumental creates the same sensation of detachment through sonic means alone: the listener is placed inside the grinding machinery without narration. The effect is arguably more disorienting.
nin-30-19.live: 10 Miles High was not a major live presence despite its intensity, making the instrumental version a rare opportunity to experience its structural qualities without the distraction of the vocal. In a live context, the stripped version might function as a purely physical, almost techno-adjacent experience of forward momentum. Its aggression without lyrics could be even more viscerally impactful in a concert setting.
nin-30-19.cultural: 10 Miles High occupied a confrontational, maximalist space on The Fragile's second disc — part of a sequence of tracks exploring rage, detachment, and dissolution. The instrumental version on Deviations strips that confrontation to its structural bones, revealing how much of the track's impact came from arrangement and texture rather than vocal performance. It's a useful reassessment of where the song's power actually lives.

nin-30-20.production: The Way Out Is Through's alternate version offers a different pass on one of The Fragile's most insistent, mantra-like pieces — a track built around relentless forward drive and grinding repetition. The alternate may feature different drum programming, arrangement, or mix choices that alter how the hypnotic momentum lands. The Nothing Studios production warmth meets the track's blunt-force repetition differently in this version.
nin-30-20.lyrics: The Way Out Is Through has one of The Fragile's most philosophically direct lyrical conceits: the only escape is complete surrender and passage through the worst of the experience. The alternate version's relationship to those lyrics — whether they're present, absent, or repositioned — shifts how that message is received. Even as an instrumental, the title's meaning permeates the music's unrelenting push.
nin-30-20.live: The Way Out Is Through became a significant live piece in NIN's catalog, its repetitive, incantatory structure making it an ideal vehicle for building and sustaining intensity. An alternate or instrumental version would function similarly, though the absence or alteration of the vocal chant would change the communal, almost ritual quality of those live performances. The song's essential momentum would remain.
nin-30-20.cultural: The phrase 'the way out is through' became something of an unofficial NIN mantra — a piece of brutal therapeutic philosophy that resonated far beyond the album's audience. The alternate version on Deviations reframes that statement in a different sonic context, asking whether the message persists without the exact delivery that made it famous. It's both a compositional exercise and an implicit question about where meaning actually lives in a song.

nin-30-21.production: Stripping the vocals from Into the Void reveals the track's thunderous low-end groove and churning guitar work in their most unadorned form — the rhythm section architecture that made the song a physical, almost funk-influenced piece becomes the entire focus. The original was already built around a brutally simple repeated riff, and the instrumental version makes that primacy absolute. Without Reznor's voice anchoring the track's emotional narrative, the music becomes entirely about forward momentum and tension.
nin-30-21.lyrics: Into the Void's original lyrics described the experience of entering a state of complete emotional and perceptual blankness — a surrender to nothingness as both terrifying and seductive. Without those words, the instrumental version evokes the void purely through sonic means: the relentless forward grind, the absence of resolution, the refusal to arrive anywhere. The void becomes the music's own structural logic.
nin-30-21.live: Into the Void was a live fixture during The Fragile touring cycle, its groove-based repetition making it a crowd-engaging centerpiece even at the heaviest moments of the set. The instrumental version would translate well to live performance, as the track's energy derives primarily from the rhythm section and guitar work rather than vocal dynamics. It's one of the Deviations instrumentals best suited to a hypothetical live performance.
nin-30-21.cultural: Into the Void stood out on The Fragile as a track with an unusually physical, low-slung groove for Reznor's work — more indebted to hard rock's body-moving tradition than industrial's cerebral detachment. The instrumental version on Deviations foregrounds that groove quality even more, presenting it almost as a purely functional exercise in propulsion. Released 17 years later, it reframes a hard rock track as something closer to a rhythmic meditation.

nin-30-22.production: Where Is Everybody?'s instrumental version removes Reznor's paranoid, isolated vocal performance and lets the song's queasy, slightly off-kilter production do all the work. The original was built on unsettling sonic disorientation — a background hum of anxiety — and stripping the vocals makes that disorientation the sole communicative mode. The Nothing Studios production, with its sense of space and subtle electronic manipulation, becomes the entire foreground.
nin-30-22.lyrics: Where Is Everybody? originally expressed profound isolation and alienation — the feeling of being stranded in a world that has emptied out around you. Without the vocal performance articulating that anxiety directly, the instrumental creates the same emotional environment through negative space and tension. The absence of a human voice actually amplifies the sense of abandonment.
nin-30-22.live: Where Is Everybody? was not a prominent live piece in The Fragile touring, its more introverted and anxious character perhaps less suited to arena-scale delivery. The instrumental version, with its stripped, uneasy character, might actually play better in a smaller or more atmospheric live context than the original. It remains a somewhat underappreciated piece even with the Deviations exposure.
nin-30-22.cultural: The opening question of the track — where is everybody? — resonated with themes of social alienation that Reznor explored throughout The Fragile, rooted in his period of isolation and substance dependency during the record's creation. The instrumental version strips even the voice asking the question, leaving only the sonic texture of that loneliness. Released in 2016, it finds renewed relevance in cultural conversations about disconnection and digital isolation.

nin-30-23.production: The Mark Has Been Made's alternate version reworks one of The Fragile's most cinematic and expansive instrumental pieces — a track that already functioned as pure mood and texture on the original album. The alternate arrangement may shift the balance between its orchestral and electronic elements, or explore different dynamics and pacing from the version that appeared on The Fragile. As a track already built on atmosphere rather than song structure, the alternate version offers a parallel window into the same emotional space.
nin-30-23.lyrics: The Mark Has Been Made was largely instrumental on The Fragile, meaning the alternate version's lyrical deviation is primarily compositional and textural. The title implies permanence, consequence, and the irreversibility of past actions — a theme deeply consistent with The Fragile's emotional universe of damage and its aftermath. The music carries that weight without needing words to articulate it.
nin-30-23.live: This piece was used atmospherically in live contexts during The Fragile era, often serving as an ambient transitional moment rather than a featured performance. The alternate version's slightly different arrangement adds a second option for how that atmospheric function could be served. Either version works as a moment of reflection and expansion within a longer set.
nin-30-23.cultural: The Mark Has Been Made holds a place in The Fragile as one of its most abstractly beautiful passages — music that functions closer to film score than to rock songwriting. Its inclusion in Deviations as an alternate version signals that Reznor considered multiple approaches to its construction before committing to the final form. The alternate represents a road not taken in the finished album's emotional architecture.

nin-30-24.production: Was It Worth It?'s instrumental strips what was already one of The Fragile's more sparsely arranged and emotionally raw pieces, removing vocal performance to leave only the song's skeletal harmonic and melodic structure. The production on the original was relatively uncluttered for a NIN track, making the instrumental version feel more like a refinement than a radical transformation. The emotional question encoded in the title hovers over the music without anyone to voice it.
nin-30-24.lyrics: The original Was It Worth It? posed its central question with exhausted resignation — had any of the suffering, the creative struggle, the personal destruction been justified by what was made? Without the vocal delivery, the instrumental version offers that question to the listener to answer for themselves through the music alone. The absence of Reznor's voice asking the question makes it more open and more haunting.
nin-30-24.live: Was It Worth It? was not a live set staple, its more reflective and emotionally spent character making it better suited to a headphone listening experience than a concert stage. The instrumental version is no more stageable but potentially more useful as a piece of ambient or background music that carries its emotional weight quietly. It's one of the Deviations pieces that functions most naturally as private listening.
nin-30-24.cultural: The question Was It Worth It? carries enormous weight as a summary of The Fragile's emotional arc — an album made during a period of destruction and recovery, explicitly about whether survival and creation justify their costs. The instrumental version on Deviations asks that question again 17 years later, from a position where Reznor had definitively answered it through his subsequent career. The retrospective frame makes the stripped version both sadder and more affirmative.

nin-30-25.production: Please's instrumental version removes the vocal pleading that gave the original its desperately exposed emotional character, leaving behind the track's swelling, moderately dense arrangement to carry the weight of supplication alone. The production on the original relied heavily on the contrast between the vulnerability of the vocal and the growing intensity of the backing, and the instrumental rebalances that dynamic entirely toward the latter. The result emphasizes the music's own capacity to convey helplessness and yearning.
nin-30-25.lyrics: Please was one of The Fragile's most direct expressions of emotional desperation — a naked request for connection, for help, for relief from isolation. Without the voice enacting that plea, the instrumental version must communicate need through dynamics and texture rather than words. The musical architecture of desperation — the building pressure, the unresolved tension — does the work that the lyrics previously articulated.
nin-30-25.live: Please appeared in some live configurations as an emotionally grounding mid-set moment, its relatively straightforward structure and urgent energy making it accessible in a concert context. The instrumental would strip that accessibility further, making it a more purely atmospheric experience. It would function best as a transitional piece in a live setting rather than a featured moment.
nin-30-25.cultural: Please fits within a lineage of NIN tracks that express vulnerability and need without irony or protective distance — a lineage that includes tracks like Hurt and The Fragile itself. The instrumental version on Deviations revisits that vulnerability through a 2016 lens, arriving after Reznor had built a stable life and career, giving the stripped music a quality of archaeological tenderness. It's the sound of someone looking back at when they needed something very badly.

nin-30-26.production: The instrumental version of +Appendage strips vocals from a track that already occupied ambiguous territory on The Fragile — the plus-sign prefixed title suggesting an addition or supplement to something larger. The production likely carries the layered, slightly clinical Nothing Studios sound, and without vocals the track's function as appendage is heightened: it exists as pure sonic extension rather than a discrete song. The stripping-back makes its connective tissue role even more explicit.
nin-30-26.lyrics: As an appendage by name, this track's lyrical content was likely minimal or ancillary on the original, so the instrumental version may not dramatically alter the experience. What changes is the removal of any human vocal presence in what was already a marginal, additive piece — doubling its quality of existing at the edges. The music becomes purely functional architecture, a passage between states.
nin-30-26.live: A track titled +Appendage was never going to be a live centerpiece, and the instrumental version only confirms its role as a transitional or atmospheric piece. Its interest lies in the sessions archaeology — the fact that Reznor and his collaborators developed it fully enough to include in the Deviations set. It's a behind-the-scenes glimpse at how The Fragile's atmosphere was built.
nin-30-26.cultural: The naming convention of +Appendage — using punctuation to signal an add-on quality — reflects the same architectural thinking that produced The Fragile as a double album with an integrated emotional logic rather than a random collection. The instrumental version in Deviations treats that appendage quality as a complete thing worth hearing in its own right, seventeen years after it was deemed supplementary. It's a small act of archival reclamation.

nin-30-27.production: The New Flesh's instrumental version removes vocals from a track whose title references David Cronenberg's Videodrome — 'long live the new flesh' — and whose musical character likely reflected the body-horror cybernetic themes that percolated through late-90s NIN aesthetics. Stripping the vocal leaves the track's sonic body exposed, which is an appropriately Cronenbergian result. The Nothing Studios production would give the track warmth even at its most mechanistic.
nin-30-27.lyrics: The New Flesh as a Cronenberg reference situates the track in a discourse about technology, transformation, and the uncomfortable merger of flesh and machine — themes that had been central to NIN since the industrial era but reached a new nuance on The Fragile. Without vocals, the instrumental lets the body-of-music speak without a human voice mediating the experience. It becomes an enactment of the concept rather than a description of it.
nin-30-27.live: The New Flesh has no significant live history, making the Deviations instrumental essentially the only public version of the piece. Its cinematic reference and probable dense, atmospheric production suggest it would work well in a film score or ambient context. As a live piece it would require substantial reimagining from its recorded state.
nin-30-27.cultural: The Videodrome reference in the title connects NIN's Fragile-era work to a lineage of art that explores the disturbing intimacy between human bodies and technological systems — a thread running from Cronenberg through industrial music to contemporary cyberpunk culture. The 2016 release of the instrumental adds a new layer: in an era of ubiquitous screens and digital embodiment, the title resonates differently than it did in 1999. Cronenberg's new flesh has arrived in ways that make the track feel unexpectedly prescient.

nin-30-28.production: Complication Alternate Version 2 represents the second of two alternate passes on the same track included in the Deviations set, suggesting Reznor generated multiple significantly different approaches to this composition during the Fragile sessions. The production differences between the two alternates and the presumed original reveal the range of sonic interpretations Reznor considered for the same piece. This multiplicity is rare in the NIN catalog and makes Complication one of the most thoroughly documented compositional processes across the Deviations release.
nin-30-28.lyrics: Having two alternate versions of the same track on Deviations means the lyrical or vocal treatment — if vocals are present in either — was also subject to revision and experimentation. The evolution across multiple versions of Complication allows listeners to trace how the emotional delivery of the same material might shift across different arrangements. Even small differences in vocal placement or production approach can radically alter how a song's meaning lands.
nin-30-28.live: Complication was not a live piece, and having two alternate versions suggests it was more of a session experiment than a finished song Reznor planned to perform. The existence of multiple alternates makes the recording session itself the event rather than any single live realization. Both versions are documents of a compositional process made public long after the fact.
nin-30-28.cultural: The release of two alternate versions of Complication in a single box set is an unusual archival decision that signals Reznor's willingness to let the messiness of the creative process become part of the public record. Most artists release one alternate and imply a cleaner narrative of artistic intent; having two alternates acknowledges that the session was genuinely exploratory and unresolved. This transparency is consistent with Reznor's increasingly open approach to discussing his creative process in the 2010s.

nin-30-29.production: Feeders is another Deviations-exclusive vault track from the Fragile sessions, never appearing on the original album and making its public debut in the 2016 box set. The production would sit in the Nothing Studios aesthetic of that period, and the title's suggestion of parasitic or dependency-based relationships fits naturally in The Fragile's thematic world. Its full arrangement and sonic character are only accessible through the Deviations release, making it one of the set's genuine archival discoveries.
nin-30-29.lyrics: As an unreleased track, Feeders carries no pre-existing fan interpretive framework — the title alone positions it within themes of codependency, exploitation, or consuming need that were central to Reznor's emotional landscape during the Fragile recording. Whether the track has vocals or is instrumental, its title does significant work in suggesting its emotional register. It's a word that implies something unhealthy about connection and sustenance.
nin-30-29.live: Feeders has never been performed live and was unknown before 2016, giving it the rare quality of a completely fresh NIN document seventeen years in the making. Its live potential depends entirely on its arrangement and energy — as a vault track it could be anything from a quiet ambient piece to something harder-edged. Without hearing it in a concert context, it exists purely as a recorded moment.
nin-30-29.cultural: The title Feeders resonates with a kind of dark ecology — things that sustain themselves through others, which maps onto themes of addiction (the habit as feeder), codependent relationships, and the creative process itself as something that consumes the artist. Released in 2016 as part of a retrospective excavation, the title also carries a retrospective quality: the past as something that continues to feed on the present. It's one of the more evocative titles in the Deviations set.

nin-30-31.production: The first of the Complication alternates in the Deviations sequencing (numbered 30-31 but titled Alternate Version rather than Alternate Version 2), this track offers yet another angle on a composition that apparently generated significant session variation. The production across this version and its companion alternate would reveal the range of sonic approaches considered for the same piece. Together, the two Complication alternates constitute an unusually deep look into the revision and variation process that characterized the Fragile sessions.
nin-30-31.lyrics: Like its companion alternate, this version of Complication offers a different treatment of whatever lyrical or vocal material the track contains, making minor or major variations in delivery, arrangement, or mix that alter the emotional experience. The existence of two alternates suggests the song's emotional resolution was contested or unclear during the sessions. Each version represents a different answer to the question of what Complication should feel like.
nin-30-31.live: Complication in any of its versions was never a live piece, remaining purely a studio entity across all incarnations. The pair of alternates in Deviations function as session documentation rather than performance texts. Their value lies in the insight they offer into Reznor's compositional process rather than any live realization.
nin-30-31.cultural: The decision to include two alternates of Complication in the Deviations box set — alongside multiple other vault-only tracks — speaks to the ambition of the release as a genuine archival document rather than a simple deluxe edition. Where most archival releases curate toward the best single take, Deviations presents the process itself as the artifact. The two Complication versions together suggest a creative conversation about the same idea that was never fully resolved, preserved in amber and released to the public in 2016.

nin-30-32.production: An alternate raw mix of 'Claustrophobia Machine' from The Fragile sessions, stripped of the polish on the final album version. The track was prepared by Trent Reznor and Atticus Ross for the 2016 Fragile: Deviations 1 release, a box set presenting instrumentals and archival session material. The rawer mix exposes the underlying drum programming and layered synth textures in a less processed state.
nin-30-32.lyrics: As a purely instrumental piece, there are no vocals or lyrics. The track communicates through dense, claustrophobic textures that mirror its title — a compression of sound space. The absence of lyrics makes the mechanical, grinding quality of the instrumentation the primary expressive vehicle.
nin-30-32.live: Neither the original 'Claustrophobia Machine' nor this raw variant has been performed live by NIN. The Fragile-era material was largely absent from setlists after the 2000 Fragility tour concluded. Deviations 1 material was considered archival rather than performance-ready.
nin-30-32.cultural: Deviations 1 arrived during a renewed interest in The Fragile's legacy, 17 years after its 1999 release. The box set was part of a wave of archival NIN releases that gave fans access to session-level material previously unavailable. It reflected the era's appetite for deluxe reissues that offered transparency into the creative process.

nin-30-33.production: Last Heard From is one of the previously unreleased instrumental pieces from The Fragile sessions included on Deviations 1. It was recorded circa 1999 during the same period as The Fragile but did not make the final cut of the double album. Reznor and Ross curated the track for the 2016 box set as part of presenting a fuller picture of the sessions.
nin-30-33.lyrics: Entirely instrumental, Last Heard From carries a melancholic, searching quality in its title that implies narrative without any verbal content. The song title itself suggests disconnection and absence — themes consistent with The Fragile's emotional universe of loss and dissociation.
nin-30-33.live: The track has never been performed live, existing solely as a studio artifact from the Deviations 1 collection. Given its status as a session outtake, it falls outside the repertoire NIN draws from for touring. Its appeal is primarily to dedicated fans exploring the full scope of The Fragile era.
nin-30-33.cultural: Released in 2016 alongside the remastered Fragile double album, Deviations 1 positioned the archival material as a companion document to one of NIN's most celebrated works. The release came during a period when Reznor was publicly revisiting his back catalog and collaborating with Ross on film scores, lending it a sense of retrospective reflection.

nin-30-35.production: This instrumental version of 'The Big Come Down' strips away Reznor's vocals to expose the densely layered industrial percussion and synth architecture beneath. The original track from The Fragile (1999) was notable for its aggressive, pounding rhythm, and the instrumental version foregrounds that mechanical brutality. Reznor and Ross prepared these alternate mixes specifically for the Deviations 1 package.
nin-30-35.lyrics: Without lyrics, the track loses the original's themes of alienation and cyclical self-destruction, but gains a kind of pure percussive abstraction. The vocal absence shifts the listening experience from confessional to purely physical — the rhythm becomes the message. Fans familiar with the original can still project the lyrical narrative onto the instrumental.
nin-30-35.live: 'The Big Come Down' appeared in NIN setlists during the 2000 Fragility 2.0 tour and was part of the live repertoire for a period. The instrumental version specifically was never performed live, as it was an archival studio construct rather than a performance arrangement. The original's live versions were known for their intense, almost violent energy.
nin-30-35.cultural: The instrumental reworkings on Deviations 1 contributed to a broader conversation about how production choices shape meaning in rock and electronic music. By removing vocals, Reznor effectively deconstructed his own songs, allowing listeners to hear the compositional skeleton. This kind of archival transparency was relatively rare for rock artists of NIN's stature in 2016.

nin-30-36.production: The instrumental version of 'Underneath It All,' the closing track of The Fragile's second disc, reveals the expansive ambient architecture Reznor built beneath the original's hushed vocals. The piece is a study in space and decay — long sustains and carefully placed sonic events. On Deviations 1, it functions almost as a meditation on The Fragile's closing emotional note.
nin-30-36.lyrics: The original 'Underneath It All' is one of the most intimate moments on The Fragile, with Reznor's voice barely above a whisper. Stripped of those vocals, the instrumental version transforms the piece into pure texture, allowing the underlying orchestration and synthesizer work to stand as its own statement. The absence of words gives it a more universal, ambient quality.
nin-30-36.live: The original 'Underneath It All' was not a regular live staple given its fragile, quiet nature. The instrumental version exists only as part of the Deviations 1 archival package and has never been performed. It remains one of the more contemplative corners of the NIN catalog.
nin-30-36.cultural: As the closer of The Fragile's second disc in original form, 'Underneath It All' always carried significant weight as the emotional resolution to a devastating double album. The instrumental version on Deviations 1 recontextualizes that emotional resolution, placing it in the company of other session variants and suggesting the album's ending could have sounded quite different.

nin-30-37.production: The instrumental version of 'Ripe (With Decay)' — already a largely instrumental piece on the original Fragile — strips away the minimal vocal elements to create a near-purely abstract sound collage. The track was one of The Fragile's most experimental pieces, featuring found sounds, manipulated noise, and ambient textures. The Deviations 1 version deepens its opacity.
nin-30-37.lyrics: The original 'Ripe (With Decay)' contained sparse, whispered vocal fragments that barely registered as lyrics in the conventional sense. Removing them entirely creates a more alien, impenetrable piece. The title's suggestion of biological decay and organic matter aligns with the track's unsettling sonic palette of corroded, decomposing sounds.
nin-30-37.live: 'Ripe (With Decay)' was never performed live in any form, as its studio-bound nature — built from layers of manipulated noise — made it essentially unplayable in a concert context. It stands as one of the most abstract entries in the NIN catalog, representing Reznor's interest in pure sound design over songcraft.
nin-30-37.cultural: As a closing piece on The Fragile's second disc, 'Ripe (With Decay)' represented NIN's furthest excursion into avant-garde territory up to that point. The Deviations 1 instrumental version situates the track within the broader context of Reznor's experimental tendencies that would later inform his film scoring work with Atticus Ross. It's a reminder of how far NIN was willing to push outside rock convention.

nin-31-01.production: The lead single from Add Violence (Halo 31, 2017), 'Less Than' opens the EP with a hard-driving, aggressive electronic rock sound that signaled a more abrasive direction following Not the Actual Events. The production by Reznor and Ross features heavy distorted synths, pounding programmed drums, and a tension between polish and rawness that defines the EP. It was released on July 13, 2017, alongside the EP announcement.
nin-31-01.lyrics: The lyrics are confrontational and politically charged, widely interpreted as a response to the political climate of 2017 — though Reznor avoided explicit statements. Lines like 'What if everything around you isn't quite as it seems?' and the repeated refrain suggest paranoia, gaslighting, and a cultural state of manufactured unreality. The song's aggression feels directed outward at systemic forces rather than inward as in earlier NIN work.
nin-31-01.live: 'Less Than' was performed live during the Cold and Black and Infinite tour in 2018, where it became a setlist staple as a high-energy opener. Its driving tempo and aggressive energy translated powerfully to the live arena context. The Cold and Black and Infinite shows paired the Add Violence material with deep cuts in large venues.
nin-31-01.cultural: Released in the charged political atmosphere of mid-2017, 'Less Than' was received as NIN's most overt political commentary in years. The music video directed by David Lynch collaborator and frequent NIN visual partner featured unsettling imagery consistent with the trilogy's aesthetic. The EP's release confirmed NIN's return to active recording after a quieter period following Hesitation Marks.

nin-31-02.production: A mid-tempo, hypnotic track on Add Violence, 'The Lovers' features layers of pulsing synthesizers, restrained drum programming, and a more spacious production approach compared to its neighbors on the EP. Reznor and Ross create a dense but controlled sonic environment where individual elements emerge and recede with care. The production has a slightly more accessible texture than the EP's harsher tracks.
nin-31-02.lyrics: The lyrics take an oblique, surrealist approach to themes of complicity, devotion, and self-betrayal. 'The Lovers' can be read as addressing a relationship with a destructive system as much as an interpersonal one — a recurring NIN ambiguity between personal and political. The imagery is dark but not nihilistic, suggesting a kind of bitter awareness of one's own participation in dysfunction.
nin-31-02.live: The track received live performances during the 2018 Cold and Black and Infinite tour, where the Add Violence material was presented in full. Its mid-tempo quality gave it a different energy in the setlist compared to the more aggressive tracks. Live versions retained the hypnotic, layered quality of the recording.
nin-31-02.cultural: Sitting in the middle of Add Violence, 'The Lovers' represents the EP's more introspective register amid its aggression. The title's reference to the Tarot card — The Lovers being associated with choice, duality, and values — is consistent with NIN's history of symbolic and esoteric references. The track adds depth to the EP's thematic concern with complicity and moral compromise.

nin-31-03.production: One of the most emotionally resonant tracks on Add Violence, 'This Isn't the Place' features a subdued, melancholic arrangement with restrained electronic textures and Reznor's voice delivered with unusual vulnerability. The production is sparse compared to surrounding tracks, with prominent piano elements and a slow build that never fully erupts. It stands out on the EP as a moment of genuine grief rather than aggression.
nin-31-03.lyrics: The lyrics are a eulogy or goodbye, widely understood to reference the 2016 death of David Bowie — with whom Reznor had a close artistic relationship — though Reznor never formally confirmed this. Lines addressing a departed figure with tenderness and the acknowledgment that 'this isn't the place' for them anymore carry unmistakable weight. If the Bowie reading is correct, it's one of NIN's most openly emotional pieces.
nin-31-03.live: The track was notably absent from many live setlists despite its emotional power, likely due to its sensitive subject matter and the challenge of performing it in a concert context. Some Cold and Black and Infinite shows included it as a moment of stillness amid the show's intensity. Its inclusion was always considered special by fans in attendance.
nin-31-03.cultural: Released the year after Bowie's death, 'This Isn't the Place' contributed to a wave of artistic tributes to Bowie from musicians who admired him. Reznor's connection to Bowie — who famously joined NIN on tour in 1995 — made the implied tribute particularly meaningful. The track represented a rare moment of unguarded public grief from an artist known for emotional armor.

nin-31-04.production: A taut, grinding industrial track, 'Not Anymore' features one of Add Violence's most aggressive production approaches with distorted, mechanized percussion and dense layers of processed noise. Reznor and Ross create a feeling of escalating pressure throughout the track, with few moments of release. It exemplifies the trilogy's darkening mood and harder electronic palette.
nin-31-04.lyrics: The lyrics are bleak and exhausted, conveying a sense of total collapse — of hope, connection, or belief in change. 'Not anymore' as a refrain functions as a renunciation, a closing off, suggesting a speaker who has passed through disillusionment into something more final. The repetition amplifies the sense of compulsive negation.
nin-31-04.live: Performed during the 2018 Cold and Black and Infinite tour, 'Not Anymore' fit naturally into the more aggressive sections of the setlist. Its relentless energy made it effective in a live context where NIN could leverage volume and physicality. The track's darkness was at home in the austere visual environment of those shows.
nin-31-04.cultural: As the penultimate track on Add Violence before the epic 'The Background World,' 'Not Anymore' serves a structural function of intensifying pressure before the finale. The political and cultural subtext of total disillusionment resonated with audiences in 2017. It's a concise, punishing piece that exemplifies the trilogy's refusal of false resolution.

nin-31-05.production: The closing and defining track of Add Violence, 'The Background World' runs over eleven minutes and ends with its final two minutes deteriorating into a repeated loop that becomes increasingly degraded and corrupted — an audio sequence that sounds like a tape physically destroying itself. Reznor and Ross build the track from a propulsive, motorik foundation before allowing it to fracture into entropic noise. The degradation effect is achieved through an actual process of repeated tape copying or digital simulation of signal corruption.
nin-31-05.lyrics: The lyrics in the first half of the track engage with themes of surveillance, unreality, and passive consumption — the 'background world' of the title suggesting the ambient information environment that shapes thought without conscious engagement. The second half's repetition and degradation make the lyrics increasingly indecipherable, as if the very medium of communication is breaking down. The dissolution mirrors the lyrical themes with formal precision.
nin-31-05.live: The full eleven-minute version was performed during the Cold and Black and Infinite tour, often as a set-closer with the degradation sequence serving as a powerful, almost uncomfortable final statement. The live version reportedly reproduced the deteriorating loop effect through controlled signal processing. Its length and intensity made it a bold choice to end a concert.
nin-31-05.cultural: 'The Background World' is widely considered the artistic peak of the NIN trilogy and one of the most formally ambitious pieces Reznor has created. The degradation sequence has been compared to William Basinski's Disintegration Loops in its meditation on entropy and recorded media decay. It cemented Add Violence's reputation as more than a stopgap release and announced the trilogy as a serious artistic statement.

nin-32-01.production: The opening track of Bad Witch (Halo 32, 2018), 'Shit Mirror' was recorded largely live in the room with minimal post-production overdubs, a radical departure from NIN's typical heavily layered studio approach. The track features distorted, raw drumming and aggressive saxophone contributions from Alessandro Cortini collaborator and Bad Witch saxophonist — the saxophone appears in a confrontational, abrasive register far from jazz convention. The live-in-room approach gives the track a physical, visceral quality.
nin-32-01.lyrics: The lyrics confront identity, self-perception, and the feedback loop of social performance — the 'shit mirror' being the distorted reflection society provides back to the individual. Reznor's vocal delivery is raw and contemptuous, matching the track's sonic aggression. The imagery draws on bodily self-disgust and the violence of self-examination.
nin-32-01.live: Performed at Cold and Black and Infinite tour dates in 2018, 'Shit Mirror' opened several shows with maximum aggression, establishing the raw, physical tone NIN was pursuing that cycle. The live version retained the track's confrontational energy and the saxophone elements were featured prominently. It became a key part of the shows' intense, almost ritualistic atmosphere.
nin-32-01.cultural: Bad Witch was initially announced and treated as an EP before being reclassified as an album for Grammy eligibility purposes, which Reznor confirmed in interviews. 'Shit Mirror' arrived at a cultural moment of intense online identity performance and social media's distorting effects on self-image, giving its themes immediate resonance. The title's directness was consistent with the political bluntness NIN adopted throughout the trilogy.

nin-32-02.production: A pummeling, mid-tempo track on Bad Witch, 'Ahead of Ourselves' features the saxophone prominently woven into the arrangement in a way that sits between melodic and purely tonal — never quite resolving into identifiable notes but adding mass to the texture. The drums were recorded live and retain a physical, slightly unruly quality. Reznor and Ross applied the record's ethos of minimal post-production to create something that sounds like a band playing in a room rather than a computer construction.
nin-32-02.lyrics: The lyrics address collective delusion, hubris, and the consequences of willful ignorance — the 'we' of the track implicated along with the subject in having gotten ahead of reality. Lines suggest political and social recklessness without pinpointing specific targets. The tone is accusatory but self-implicating, consistent with NIN's refusal of simple moral positioning.
nin-32-02.live: Featured in the 2018 Cold and Black and Infinite tour setlists, 'Ahead of Ourselves' translated well to the live setting given its physical drum performance and the forward momentum of its arrangement. The track occupied a mid-set position that maintained intensity without the extreme aggression of the opening songs. Live versions matched the record closely.
nin-32-02.cultural: Bad Witch as a whole arrived at the end of NIN's 2016-2018 trilogy — Not the Actual Events, Add Violence, Bad Witch — and 'Ahead of Ourselves' exemplifies the trilogy's preoccupation with a society in willful denial. The saxophone's prominent role across the EP signaled Reznor's deliberate move away from electronic production conventions and toward more organic, band-oriented sounds.

nin-32-03.production: An almost entirely instrumental piece on Bad Witch, 'Play the Goddamned Part' is built around saxophone improvisation and live drums with minimal electronic processing. The track functions as a brief, aggressive interlude rather than a developed song structure — abrupt, confrontational, over before it fully begins. It's one of the most radical departures from traditional NIN aesthetics on the record.
nin-32-03.lyrics: The sparse vocal content consists largely of the title phrase delivered as a command or threat. 'Play the goddamned part' functions as an imperative directed at a performer, a conformist, or potentially the listener — commanding compliance with a role one might not have chosen. The brevity of the lyrical content amplifies its menace.
nin-32-03.live: Given its short, fragmentary nature, 'Play the Goddamned Part' was not a consistent part of live setlists during the Cold and Black and Infinite tour. Its interlude-like quality on the record made it difficult to deploy as a standalone live piece. Where Bad Witch material was performed, the track's saxophone improvisational character made it adaptable.
nin-32-03.cultural: The track represents Bad Witch's most confrontational embrace of free improvisation and jazz-adjacent noise, placing NIN briefly in conversation with avant-garde rock and no wave traditions. The command in the title resonates with critiques of performance culture and authenticity — themes running through the entire trilogy. Its abrasiveness served as a palate cleanser that set up the more expansive tracks around it.

nin-32-04.production: Perhaps the most unexpected track in the NIN catalog, 'God Break Down the Door' is dominated by an Bowie-influenced saxophone lead and features production that deliberately evokes the Berlin Trilogy-era avant-rock sound of Low and 'Heroes.' The track was widely noted as an overt homage to David Bowie, with Reznor's vocal delivery and the saxophone arrangement echoing Bowie's late 1970s work. The live-in-room recording ethos of Bad Witch gives it a raw, physical presence.
nin-32-04.lyrics: The lyrics invoke supplication, desperation, and a plea for divine intervention or catastrophic change — 'God break down the door' as a request for some external force to shatter an intolerable situation. The imagery is apocalyptic but carries a note of hope, distinguishing it from the trilogy's more nihilistic moments. Many listeners hear the track as another tribute to Bowie, connecting the spiritual supplication to grief.
nin-32-04.live: Performed during Cold and Black and Infinite tour dates in 2018, 'God Break Down the Door' was one of the most distinctive moments in the setlist given its saxophone-forward arrangement and Bowie homage quality. Live performances emphasized the track's emotional directness. It stood as proof that NIN's live configuration in 2018 could accommodate instrumental depth beyond guitar-synth-drums.
nin-32-04.cultural: The Bowie connection was so apparent in 'God Break Down the Door' that it became one of the most discussed tracks of NIN's 2018 release cycle — Reznor's way of processing grief through aesthetic homage rather than explicit statement. Combined with the implied Bowie tribute in 'This Isn't the Place' on Add Violence, it suggests a sustained act of mourning across the trilogy. The track also confirmed that saxophone — an instrument largely absent from rock production in 2018 — could be central to a NIN release.

nin-32-06.production: The closing track of Bad Witch and the NIN trilogy, 'Over and Out' is an expansive, slowly evolving piece that builds from sparse, somber piano and low synthesizer tones into a dense, enveloping sound world over its seven-plus minute duration. The production allows the track to breathe at a pace unusual for NIN, with space treated as an active compositional element. It functions as an extended coda to the entire 2016-2018 trilogy.
nin-32-06.lyrics: Largely wordless or near-instrumental, the sparse vocal elements in 'Over and Out' carry a sense of finality and exhausted release. The title's dual meaning — a radio sign-off and a statement of completion — frames the track as a formal ending, a transmission closing. The emotional register is not triumphant but profoundly weary, suggesting survival rather than victory.
nin-32-06.live: In concert during the Cold and Black and Infinite tour, 'Over and Out' served as a closing statement that allowed shows to end in reflection rather than explosion — an unusual choice for a band known for intense finales. The extended duration was preserved in live settings, giving audiences time to decompress. It represented a mature willingness to end on ambiguity.
nin-32-06.cultural: As the concluding track of NIN's trilogy and of Bad Witch, 'Over and Out' carries considerable weight as an artistic statement of completion. The trilogy as a whole — from the raw aggression of Not the Actual Events through the formal experimentation of Add Violence and the organic rawness of Bad Witch — traced a coherent arc, and 'Over and Out' provides its resolution. Critics noted the irony that NIN's most politically engaged period ended with exhausted silence rather than a call to action.

nin-33-01.production: The opening track of Ghosts V: Together (Halo 33, 2020), 'Letting Go While Holding On' establishes the album's warm, ambient, piano-led aesthetic immediately. Reznor and Ross produced both Ghosts V: Together and its companion VI: Locusts and released them simultaneously on March 27, 2020 — the first full day of widespread COVID-19 lockdowns in the US. The production is gentle and unhurried, with soft synthesizer textures layering beneath a contemplative piano figure.
nin-33-01.lyrics: Entirely instrumental, the track communicates through its title and sonic texture rather than words. 'Letting Go While Holding On' captures a psychological paradox central to the album's emotional theme — the simultaneous necessity of release and connection during isolation. The title alone was widely felt to articulate something profound about the specific experience of the March 2020 moment.
nin-33-01.live: The Ghosts V material has not been performed live, consistent with the ambient nature of the Ghosts series as a whole. The 2020 releases were conceived as music for the specific circumstances of pandemic isolation, making live performance less relevant to their intended function. Their legacy is as recorded environmental music rather than stage repertoire.
nin-33-01.cultural: Released free of charge on March 27, 2020, Ghosts V: Together arrived at one of the most disorienting moments in recent cultural history. The simultaneous release with the darker companion piece Ghosts VI: Locusts offered listeners a choice of emotional registers — warmth or dread — that itself felt meaningful. 'Letting Go While Holding On' became a touchstone title for those seeking language to describe the early lockdown experience.

nin-33-02.production: The title track of Ghosts V: Together features layered piano, warm synthesizer pads, and a gently pulsing rhythm that suggests forward movement without urgency. Reznor and Ross achieve a balance of intimacy and openness that characterizes the album's best moments. The production quality is impeccable despite the album's ambient, understated nature — every element is precisely placed.
nin-33-02.lyrics: As an instrumental piece, 'Together' communicates its emotional content entirely through musical texture and the resonance of its title. Released during the early days of a global pandemic that had physically separated people, the title 'Together' carried enormous weight as both aspiration and comfort. The track's warmth functions as an embrace — a sonic statement of communal connection across physical distance.
nin-33-02.live: Like the rest of the Ghosts V material, 'Together' has not been performed live and was not conceived with live performance in mind. It exists as recorded environmental music. The irony of a track called 'Together' being created and consumed in isolation was noted by many listeners and reviewers.
nin-33-02.cultural: The title track of Together arrived as the world was confronting the sudden, forced isolation of pandemic lockdown — the album's emotional message of warmth and communal resilience took on immediate cultural significance. Reznor and Ross's decision to release both Ghosts V and VI for free was widely praised as a generous artistic gesture at a moment of collective anxiety. 'Together' became representative of how art could respond to crisis with care rather than exploitation.

nin-33-03.production: A more rhythmically active piece within the ambient context of Ghosts V, 'Out in the Open' features a prominent pulse and more textural movement than the album's quieter tracks. The production maintains the warm, analog-adjacent quality of Together while introducing a sense of forward momentum and expansiveness. It suggests emergence and space — the 'open' of the title rendered sonically.
nin-33-03.lyrics: Instrumental throughout, 'Out in the Open' communicates through its title and dynamic arc. The music builds an impression of space and light — a contrast to the enclosed, interior quality of lockdown life. As a compositional statement, it offers listeners a kind of mental release into imagined open spaces during a period of physical confinement.
nin-33-03.live: Not performed live, consistent with the Ghosts series' nature as studio-based ambient music. The track's expansive quality might translate interestingly to a live setting, but Reznor and Ross have not revisited the Ghosts V material for concerts.
nin-33-03.cultural: Within the context of March 2020 lockdowns, 'Out in the Open' offered an emotional escape hatch — music that invited listeners to imaginatively inhabit open, unconstrained spaces at a time when such spaces were inaccessible. The track participated in a broader cultural phenomenon of artists providing virtual access to landscapes, nature, and freedom during confinement.

nin-33-04.production: One of the more overtly emotional tracks on Ghosts V: Together, 'With Faith' features a tender piano melody at its core surrounded by delicate synthesizer textures and a careful spatial production. Reznor and Ross use dynamic subtlety — the way individual notes are allowed to sustain and decay — as primary expressive tools. The production has a devotional, almost sacred quality.
nin-33-04.lyrics: An instrumental piece whose title carries significant weight in context. 'With Faith' in early pandemic conditions spoke to a kind of non-denominational trust in human resilience and collective endurance. The music's gentle but firm quality embodies the emotional content of the title — not naive optimism but a sustained, effortful belief in positive outcomes.
nin-33-04.live: Not performed live. The devotional, intimate quality of 'With Faith' makes it a deeply personal listening experience that may not translate well to large concert settings. It represents the quieter, most interior side of NIN's musical range.
nin-33-04.cultural: Released in a moment of profound uncertainty about the near future, 'With Faith' offered something unusual for NIN: unironic, unguarded hope. Critics and fans noted that Reznor — whose career was largely built on nihilism, aggression, and self-laceration — was offering comfort and faith as genuine artistic propositions. The track marked a genuine evolution in NIN's emotional register.

nin-33-05.production: The closing track of Ghosts V: Together, 'Apart' ends the album on a bittersweet note that acknowledges the reality of separation even within the album's hopeful framework. The production is spare and spacious, with piano and synthesizer elements given maximum room. Reznor and Ross close the album with restraint rather than resolution, allowing the final notes to fade into silence without providing easy comfort.
nin-33-05.lyrics: Instrumental, with a title that introduces productive tension into the album's theme of togetherness. 'Apart' as a closing title acknowledges the truth of the moment — that despite aspirations toward connection, people were physically separated. The pairing of Together and Apart as an album's bookends reflects a sophisticated understanding that honest comfort must acknowledge reality rather than deny it.
nin-33-05.live: Not performed live. As the closing statement of one of NIN's most contextually specific releases, 'Apart' exists as part of the album's emotional arc rather than as a standalone piece. Its meaning is tied to the sequence and the moment of release.
nin-33-05.cultural: Ending Ghosts V: Together with 'Apart' was a compositionally and emotionally intelligent choice — it resisted the sentimentality of a neat resolution and acknowledged the genuine difficulty of the moment. The album's free release, its immediate cultural resonance, and its emotional honesty made it one of the most discussed NIN releases in years despite being ambient instrumental music. 'Apart' ensures the album closes with honesty intact, a final act of care for the listener.

nin-33-06.production: A gentle ambient piece built around warm, flowing synthesizer pads and soft piano motifs, characteristic of the Ghosts V: Together aesthetic. Reznor and Ross craft a texture of quiet reassurance, with subtle layering that gives the track an intimate, breath-like quality. The production sits closer to traditional ambient composition than industrial noise, continuing the duo's established Ghosts palette in a more emotionally open register.
nin-33-06.lyrics: Like the rest of Ghosts V, 'Your Touch' is an instrumental composition with no traditional lyrics. The title itself functions as the lyrical statement, suggesting warmth, physical presence, and connection — themes that resonate acutely given the album's COVID-era release when human touch had become limited by pandemic restrictions.
nin-33-06.live: As an ambient instrumental from the Ghosts series, 'Your Touch' has not been performed live. The Ghosts albums have never formed part of NIN's touring setlists, and the fragile, textural nature of this piece makes it especially suited to private listening rather than stadium presentation.
nin-33-06.cultural: Released on March 26, 2020, just as global COVID-19 lockdowns were being implemented, Ghosts V: Together landed with remarkable cultural timing. The warmth of 'Your Touch' felt like a direct emotional response to the sudden loss of physical closeness during the pandemic, and Reznor made the album available for free as a gesture of solidarity during the crisis.

nin-33-07.production: Built on a foundation of slowly evolving synth textures and delicate melodic fragments, 'Hope We Can Again' carries the most openly optimistic tone of the Ghosts V suite. Reznor and Ross employ restrained dynamics, allowing negative space to carry emotional weight alongside the sound itself. The production reflects the album's overarching philosophy of finding warmth and continuity in crisis.
nin-33-07.lyrics: As an instrumental, the track communicates entirely through its evocative title. 'Hope We Can Again' implies a longing for future restoration — of gatherings, relationships, or normalcy — and functions as an implicit lyric about collective grief and anticipation. The title's grammatical incompleteness ('hope we can again' — do what?) renders it universally applicable to whatever the listener has lost.
nin-33-07.live: Not performed live; the Ghosts instrumental series has remained exclusively a studio and home-listening phenomenon throughout its existence. Reznor has never integrated Ghosts compositions into NIN concert performances.
nin-33-07.cultural: The title captured the collective mood of early 2020 with precision, expressing a kind of cautious hopefulness amid deep uncertainty. It was part of the same-day digital release package that made Ghosts V available for free download, reflecting Reznor's decision to offer something meaningful at no cost to listeners struggling through the pandemic.

nin-33-08.production: The closing track of Ghosts V: Together, 'Still Right Here' serves as a gentle resolution to the album's emotional arc. Piano plays a central role alongside sustained synthesizer drones, giving the track a contemplative, almost classical feel. It is among the most emotionally direct pieces in the Ghosts V suite, serving as a kind of farewell and reassurance simultaneously.
nin-33-08.lyrics: As an instrumental, the title 'Still Right Here' does the lyrical work, functioning as a statement of persistent presence and endurance. In the context of pandemic-era isolation, the phrase resonates as both comfort and survival statement — I have not gone anywhere, I am still here. It bookends the Together album's thesis of connection maintained through absence.
nin-33-08.live: Like all Ghosts tracks, 'Still Right Here' has not appeared in any NIN live show. The Ghosts catalog exists in a distinct space from NIN's concert identity and has remained there throughout multiple touring cycles.
nin-33-08.cultural: As the closer of the Together half of Ghosts V–VI, the track anchors the warmer, more reassuring disc before the companion piece Ghosts VI: Locusts shifts to darker territory. The juxtaposition was intentional — Reznor and Ross conceived the two volumes as emotional opposites released simultaneously, asking listeners to sit with both hope and dread at once.

nin-34-01.production: Opening Ghosts VI: Locusts, 'The Cursed Clock' immediately establishes a more unsettling sonic environment than its Ghosts V counterpart. Ticking and mechanical rhythmic elements evoke temporal dread, while dissonant synthesizer layers create an atmosphere of mounting anxiety. The track functions as a tonal reset after the warmth of Together, signaling that Locusts will explore more psychologically troubling territory.
nin-34-01.lyrics: An instrumental whose title invokes time as a source of torment rather than comfort. 'The Cursed Clock' suggests a perception of time as oppressive — the opposite of hope — which directly reflects pandemic-era experiences of days blurring together and time feeling simultaneously frozen and relentlessly advancing. The title anchors the album's thematic preoccupation with fear, uncertainty, and inescapable awareness.
nin-34-01.live: Not performed live. Ghosts VI: Locusts, like all entries in the Ghosts series, has remained a studio-only experience and has not been integrated into NIN's touring catalog.
nin-34-01.cultural: Released on the same day as Ghosts V: Together (March 26, 2020), Ghosts VI: Locusts was conceived as a deliberate tonal counterweight. While Together offered solace, Locusts was designed to validate anxiety and dread — both emotional realities of early pandemic life. 'The Cursed Clock' functions as the entry point into that more psychologically fraught headspace.

nin-34-02.production: A slowly unfolding ambient piece characterized by creeping, low-end synthesizer textures and a pervasive sense of unease. 'Around Every Corner' uses dynamic restraint and gradual timbral shifts to sustain tension without overt aggression, placing it in the more subtle tradition of industrial ambient. Reznor and Ross allow the track to simmer rather than explode, which makes the anxiety feel inescapable rather than confrontational.
nin-34-02.lyrics: Purely instrumental, with the title evoking paranoia and hypervigilance — the psychological state of expecting threat to emerge from any direction. In 2020, this resonated with both pandemic anxiety (the virus being invisible and omnipresent) and broader social unrest. The phrase 'around every corner' encodes a specific kind of exhausting, constant alertness.
nin-34-02.live: Not performed live. As with all Ghosts instrumentals, the track exists exclusively in its recorded form and has not appeared on any NIN setlist.
nin-34-02.cultural: Part of the Locusts volume released free to the public in March 2020, the track's ambient paranoia mapped onto the early pandemic atmosphere when reliable information was scarce and danger felt genuinely invisible. Its companion release alongside the warmer Together tracks illustrated Reznor and Ross's interest in presenting emotional complexity rather than a single mood.

nin-34-03.production: The track's title implies a rhythmic structure, and Reznor and Ross oblige with a more metrically defined piece than many of the Locusts ambient excursions. Waltz time signatures are filtered through electronic production, creating a disorienting, slightly carnival-esque quality. The 'worriment' descriptor perfectly captures the track's nervous energy — there is movement, but it is anxious and circular rather than purposeful.
nin-34-03.lyrics: An instrumental in which the title functions as a kind of invented compound word — 'worriment' being an archaic or dialectal term for anxiety and worry. The waltz frame adds a darkly ironic formal elegance to the anxiety, suggesting worrying as an almost ritualistic, even graceful, compulsion. It's among the more conceptually precise titles on Locusts.
nin-34-03.live: Not performed live. The Ghosts series has never been part of NIN's concert presentations, and the intricate textural nature of Locusts tracks makes them particularly suited to studio listening.
nin-34-03.cultural: Released as part of the free COVID-era package in March 2020, the track's sense of anxious circular motion resonated with the experience of worry loops many people entered during early pandemic lockdowns. The archaic 'worriment' carries a slightly literary quality that distances the title from simple description, adding intellectual texture to the emotional content.

nin-34-04.production: One of the more kinetic pieces on Ghosts VI: Locusts, 'Run Like Hell' incorporates a driven rhythmic pulse beneath its dark ambient textures, lending the track a sense of propulsion and urgency that much of the album lacks. This is not a Pink Floyd cover but shares a conceptual space with flight and desperation. Reznor and Ross deploy the rhythm strategically, making the track feel like a genuine sonic chase.
nin-34-04.lyrics: A wordless instrumental whose title delivers the most direct imperative statement on the album. 'Run Like Hell' is an unambiguous instruction — flee, escape, don't stop. In the context of Locusts' pandemic-era anxiety, it can be read as a primal response to pervasive threat, though it is impossible to run from an invisible virus, adding a note of futility to the urgency.
nin-34-04.live: Not performed live. The distinction between NIN as a live rock act and NIN as ambient electronic composers (the Ghosts mode) has been maintained throughout the project's existence.
nin-34-04.cultural: Released free in March 2020, the track's urgency resonated with the disorienting early days of the pandemic when advice changed rapidly and existential threat felt immediate. The title's directness contrasts with the more abstract ambient titles on Locusts, making it stand out as a moment of unmediated panic within the album's more textural dread.

nin-34-05.production: A more spacious, dissociative piece within the Locusts sequence, characterized by floating synthesizer textures and a detached, dreamlike quality. The parenthetical in the title — '(Don't Mind Me)' — is unusual and suggests a self-effacing or dissociated narrator. Production choices emphasize distance and unreality, creating a sense of witnessing events rather than inhabiting them.
nin-34-05.lyrics: The full title 'When It Happens (Don't Mind Me)' is among the more haunting on Locusts, suggesting a passive witness who asks not to be noticed while something unnamed occurs. This dissociative stance can be read as psychological withdrawal under stress, emotional numbing, or an oblique reference to death. The parenthetical undermines the gravity of 'when it happens' with a casual dismissal, creating tonal dissonance.
nin-34-05.live: Not performed live. Consistent with the entire Ghosts catalog, this track has not been incorporated into any NIN touring production.
nin-34-05.cultural: In the context of the COVID-19 pandemic's early mortality and uncertainty, the title's dissociative quality resonates with the experience of encountering news of mass death at a remove — wanting to step aside from the enormity of what was happening. Released for free alongside Together and the rest of Locusts on March 26, 2020.

nin-34-06.production: A crash-and-wreckage sonic metaphor rendered in electronic ambient textures, 'Another Crashed Car' deploys abrasive and disrupted sound design to evoke impact and aftermath. The track is among the more chaotic on Locusts, using noise elements alongside the album's characteristic dark ambient palette. The 'another' in the title suggests repetition and accumulation — this is not the first, and won't be the last.
nin-34-06.lyrics: Instrumental, with a title that evokes trauma as pattern rather than isolated incident. The word 'another' carries enormous weight, suggesting a speaker so accustomed to catastrophe that individual disasters no longer register as unique events. In the pandemic context this reads as the numbing effect of daily death tolls — each one distinct, yet collectively processed as one more instance of a terrible routine.
nin-34-06.live: Not performed live. Like all Ghosts instrumentals, 'Another Crashed Car' exists solely in its recorded, home-listening form.
nin-34-06.cultural: Part of the free pandemic release, the track's title resonates with 2020's sequential crises — not only the pandemic but the social and political upheavals running concurrently. The vehicle crash metaphor is a classic NIN image of industrial modernity and bodily vulnerability dating back to earlier work, here deployed in ambient rather than aggressive form.

nin-34-07.production: A more subdued, deliberately incomplete-feeling track, 'Temp Fix' uses sparse textures and unresolved harmonic material to suggest impermanence and improvised solutions. The title's abbreviation of 'temporary' is mirrored in production choices that avoid closure or conventional resolution. Reznor and Ross keep the track intentionally thin, as if the musical structure itself is a patch rather than a solution.
nin-34-07.lyrics: An instrumental whose title encodes the pragmatic psychology of coping under crisis — not solving problems but applying temporary patches to keep functioning. 'Temp Fix' captures the psychological mode of triage and short-term survival thinking that characterized life during early pandemic lockdowns. The title's casual informality makes it feel everyday and human rather than dramatic.
nin-34-07.live: Not performed live. The Ghosts catalog has consistently remained separate from NIN's live touring identity throughout the series.
nin-34-07.cultural: In 2020, 'Temp Fix' resonated as a description of nearly every solution being applied to pandemic challenges — emergency policy, improvised PPE, work-from-home arrangements, makeshift mental health strategies. Released for free on March 26, 2020, it captured the bricolage quality of collective crisis management.

nin-34-08.production: One of the more emotionally affecting tracks on Locusts, 'Trust Fades' uses slow, dissolving synthesizer textures to evoke gradual erosion rather than sudden collapse. The production sustains a mournful quality throughout, with harmonic material that decays over the course of the piece as if illustrating its own title. Reznor and Ross show restraint here, letting the feeling of loss accumulate through subtraction rather than addition.
nin-34-08.lyrics: An instrumental in which the title captures a specific psychological process — trust not breaking but eroding imperceptibly over time. In 2020, this resonated with eroding trust in institutions, media, government, and perhaps interpersonal relationships strained by isolation. The title avoids drama in favor of a quiet, sad observation about change.
nin-34-08.live: Not performed live. Ghosts VI: Locusts has not been incorporated into any NIN live performance in any form.
nin-34-08.cultural: Released during a period of acute institutional stress in March 2020, 'Trust Fades' anticipated the deepening of distrust in public health systems, media, and political leadership that would intensify throughout the pandemic year. As a piece of ambient music, its non-confrontational approach to this theme arguably makes it more unsettling than an aggressive statement would.

nin-34-09.production: The title's bluntness is matched by a production approach that is among the most unadorned on Locusts — spare, dark, and without redemptive harmonic movement. 'A Really Bad Night' strips back Reznor and Ross's ambient vocabulary to its most essential elements, using sustained dissonance and minimal textural variation to evoke a psychological state of being at the bottom. The informality of 'really bad' grounds the abstraction in colloquial human experience.
nin-34-09.lyrics: Purely instrumental, with a title that deliberately uses ordinary language rather than poetic abstraction to name a universally recognizable experience. 'A Really Bad Night' doesn't reach for metaphor; it simply states the condition. This informality is its most pointed quality — it sounds like something you'd text a friend, not a track title from an ambient industrial album.
nin-34-09.live: Not performed live. The Ghosts series has remained exclusively in the studio/home-listening domain throughout its existence.
nin-34-09.cultural: In March 2020, amid pandemic terror, economic collapse, and social upheaval, 'a really bad night' could describe almost any given evening for vast numbers of people. By naming the experience in such a familiar, understated way, the track invited listeners to recognize their own distress rather than aestheticizing it. Part of the free release package on March 26, 2020.

nin-34-10.production: A track whose title carries heavy ironic resonance, 'Your New Normal' employs disorienting, unsettled ambient textures that resist resolution — an apt production philosophy for a piece about the forced acceptance of changed circumstances. The music doesn't settle into comfort even when it could, maintaining an undercurrent of wrongness beneath whatever surface calm exists. Reznor and Ross embed the irony of 'new normal' into the production itself.
nin-34-10.lyrics: Instrumental, though the title was arguably the most prescient phrase on the entire Ghosts V–VI release given that 'new normal' became the dominant rhetorical frame for pandemic life in 2020. Released in March 2020, the track effectively named the psychosocial phenomenon before it became ubiquitous.
